attributes-and-init.label: Attributes and __init__
attributes-and-init.summary: Initialize object state with constructor parameters.

try_attributes_and_init_sketch0.title: Try it yourself
try_attributes_and_init_sketch0.prompt: Start small: create a `Car` class that remembers two basic details, `brand` and `year`, as soon as the object is created.

try_attributes_and_init_sketch1.title: Try it yourself
try_attributes_and_init_sketch1.prompt: Now make the object hold a little more related data. Define `Player` so it stores `username`, `score`, and `level` on each new instance.

try_attributes_and_init_sketch2.title: Try it yourself
try_attributes_and_init_sketch2.prompt: Finish the ladder by using stored attributes in behavior. Create `Rectangle`, save `width` and `height`, and add `area()` so the object can calculate its own area.

ex1.title: Create a Car class with attributes
ex1.prompt: Start small: create a `Car` class that remembers two basic details, `brand` and `year`, as soon as the object is created.
ex1.hint: Give `Car` a constructor, then save each incoming value onto the instance.

help.concept: A constructor is where a new object picks up its starting state. Two inputs should become two attributes on `self`.
help.hint_2: Use one assignment for the brand and one for the year.

ex2.title: What __init__ is for
ex2.prompt: What is the main purpose of `__init__` in a Python class?
ex2.hint: Think about what happens right when a new object is created.

help.concept: `__init__` runs when an instance is created and is commonly used to give that object its starting attribute values.
help.hint_1: It is not used to import modules or print automatically.
help.hint_2: Choose the option about setting up a new object's starting data.

options.a: To set up an object's initial attributes
options.b: To rename the class after creation
options.c: To import code from another file

ex3.title: Complete an attribute assignment
ex3.prompt: Choose the missing code that stores the incoming `name` parameter on the object.
ex3.hint: The left side should be the object's attribute, not just the parameter name.

help.concept: To save constructor input on an object, assign the parameter to an attribute on `self`. The `self.` part means the value belongs to that instance.
help.hint_1: You want the object to remember `name` later as an attribute.
help.hint_2: The missing code should be the attribute target on the left side of `=`.

ex4.title: Order the constructor steps
ex4.prompt: Put these steps in the order that matches creating an object with stored attributes.
ex4.hint: Start with the class definition, then the constructor, then the assignments, then object creation.

help.concept: A class is defined first, then `__init__` describes how new instances store data, and finally an instance is created using constructor arguments.
help.hint_1: The assignments inside `__init__` happen before you can use the created object.
help.hint_2: The object creation call comes after the class and constructor code are written.

ex5.title: Pick the real attributes
ex5.prompt: A class `Movie` uses `__init__(self, title, year)` and assigns `self.title = title` and `self.year = year`. Which names are attributes on each `Movie` instance? Choose all that apply.
ex5.hint: Look for the names attached to `self.` inside the assignments.

help.concept: Attributes are the names stored on the object with `self.attribute_name`. Constructor parameter names by themselves are not automatically attributes unless assigned to `self`.
help.hint_1: Check the left side of each assignment in `__init__`.
help.hint_2: The correct choices are the names that would work in expressions like `movie.name_here`.

ex6.title: Store three values in a Player class
ex6.prompt: Now make the object hold a little more related data. Define `Player` so it stores `username`, `score`, and `level` on each new instance.
ex6.hint: This is the same pattern as before, just with one more attribute to save.

help.concept: Once you know how to save one or two constructor values, the next step is repeating that pattern for a larger group of related data.
help.hint_1: Each parameter should have a matching attribute on `self`.
help.hint_2: If you count three incoming values, you should also have three attribute assignments.

ex7.title: Which line creates an attribute
ex7.prompt: Inside `__init__(self, color)`, which line creates an attribute named `color` on the object?
ex7.hint: An attribute belongs to the object, so the line must use `self.`.

help.concept: Attributes are stored on the instance using `self.attribute_name = value`. A plain variable assignment does not attach data to the object.
help.hint_1: Look for the option that puts `color` after `self.` on the left side.
help.hint_2: The correct line stores the parameter value inside the object so `obj.color` will work later.

ex8.title: Build a class from parameters to attributes
ex8.prompt: Arrange the code pieces into a correct class definition that stores `title` on a `Song` object.
ex8.hint: Start with the class line, then the constructor line, then the indented assignment.

help.concept: A valid class definition needs the class header first, then the `__init__` method, then the attribute assignment inside that method.
help.hint_1: The assignment must be nested inside `__init__`, not above it.
help.hint_2: The constructor line should come right after the class line.

ex9.title: Choose the missing constructor parameter use
ex9.prompt: Fill the blank so the object stores the incoming `pages` value in its `pages` attribute.
ex9.hint: The right side should be the parameter value being passed into `__init__`.

help.concept: In `self.the missing term = the missing term`, the left side is the object's attribute and the right side is the constructor parameter value being copied into that attribute.
help.hint_1: You are choosing the value that should go on the right side of `=`.
help.hint_2: Pick the parameter name, not `self.the missing term` again and not a print call.

ex10.title: Find the true statements about __init__
ex10.prompt: Which statements about `__init__` are true? Choose all that apply.
ex10.hint: Use the lesson explanation and the wording of this question to narrow the answer.

help.concept: This support text was repaired because the original wording revealed the answer too directly.
help.hint_1: Compare the question to the lesson example and remove details that do not match the requested concept.
help.hint_2: Use the role or evidence named in the prompt instead of relying on answer wording.

options.a: `__init__` runs when a new instance is created
options.b: `self` refers to the specific object being initialized
options.c: `__init__` is only used to print text
options.d: `self` is another name for the class itself in every case

ex11.title: Add a method that uses initialized attributes
ex11.prompt: Finish the ladder by using stored attributes in behavior. Create `Rectangle`, save `width` and `height`, and add `area()` so the object can calculate its own area.
ex11.hint: Store the two dimensions first, then use those stored values inside the method.

help.concept: This step combines constructor setup with object behavior. The method should not use unrelated variables; it should read the rectangle's own saved dimensions.
help.hint_1: Inside `area`, read the width and height from `self`.
help.hint_2: Return their product instead of printing it.

sb1.title: What __init__ does
sb1.bodyMarkdown:
When you create an object, Python can run a special method named `__init__`. Its job is to set up the new object's starting data, called **attributes**.

Here is a simple example:

```python
class Dog:
    def __init__(self, name, age):
        self.name = name
        self.age = age

my_dog = Dog("Milo", 3)
print(my_dog.name)
print(my_dog.age)
```

Step by step:

- `class Dog:` starts a new class.
- `def __init__(self, name, age):` defines the constructor.
- `self.name = name` stores the given `name` inside the object.
- `self.age = age` stores the given `age` inside the object.
- `Dog("Milo", 3)` creates one `Dog` object.
- `my_dog.name` reads the object's `name` attribute.

So after creation, this object remembers its own data.

**Try it yourself:** Change `"Milo"` to another name and `3` to another age. Click Run and check the output panel. Then try adding `print(type(my_dog))` to see that Python created a `Dog` object.

sb2.title: Attributes belong to each instance
sb2.bodyMarkdown:
An **instance** is one object made from a class. Each instance can have different attribute values, even when they come from the same class.

```python
class Book:
    def __init__(self, title, pages):
        self.title = title
        self.pages = pages

book1 = Book("Python Basics", 120)
book2 = Book("Space Stories", 200)

print(book1.title)
print(book2.title)
```

This works because:

- `book1` and `book2` are different instances.
- Both have a `title` attribute.
- The values are different because each object got different constructor arguments.

A helpful way to think about it: the class is the blueprint, and each instance is a separate thing built from that blueprint.

**Your turn:** Add `print(book1.pages)` and `print(book2.pages)`. Then change one book's values and run again.

Line by line: read each statement from top to bottom. Notice which names are created, which values are passed into functions, and what output the code is meant to show.

sb3.title: Why we use self.attribute = parameter
sb3.bodyMarkdown:
Inside `__init__`, you usually copy incoming parameter values into attributes on `self`.

```python
class Player:
    def __init__(self, username, score):
        self.username = username
        self.score = score
```

Line by line:

- `username` is a parameter passed into the constructor.
- `self.username` is an attribute stored on the object.
- `self.username = username` means “save the given username inside this object.”

If you only use `username = username`, nothing gets stored on the object. The object needs `self.` to keep that data for later.

For example, this lets you do:

```python
p = Player("Ava", 10)
print(p.username)
```

Without `self.username`, `p.username` would not exist.

**Try this:** Add a third attribute such as `level`, pass it into `__init__`, and print it from an instance.
